name: Bahram Gur
description: Bahram Gur, also known as Bahram V, was a legendary Sasanian king of Persia (r. 420–438 CE) celebrated for his bravery, hunting prowess, and romantic adventures. His father, Yazdegerd I, was a controversial ruler, and Bahram's early life was spent in Arabia, where he was raised by the Mundhir king. Upon Yazdegerd's death, the Persian nobles, initially wary of Bahram, eventually invited him back to claim his throne after a trial by fire. Bahram proved his legitimacy through his strength and skill, solidifying his rule. He is renowned for his hunting expeditions, particularly his legendary feats of killing lions and onagers (gurs), which earned him the epithet 'Gur' (meaning onager). He is also remembered for his love for Azadeh, a musician and harpist, and the tragic story of her death during a hunt. Bahram Gur is a symbol of ideal kingship, embodying courage, justice, and cultural patronage. His reign is often depicted in Persian art and literature, solidifying his place as a central figure in Persian mythology and a romanticized image of the Sasanian era. His stories are filled with chivalry, adventure, and a strong connection to the natural world, making him an enduringly popular figure.
subtitle: The Hunter King of Persia
achievements: Claiming the throne through trial by fire
Bringing prosperity and stability to Persia

keyMyths.title: The Trial by Fire
keyMyths.description: To prove his legitimacy, Bahram Gur underwent a trial by fire, successfully emerging unharmed and demonstrating his divine right to rule. This event solidified his position as the rightful king of Persia. The trial involved walking between two huge fires.

keyMyths.title: The Hunt with Azadeh
keyMyths.description: During a hunt, Bahram Gur, showing off his skill, pinned a male and female gazelle together with one arrow. Azadeh criticized his actions, leading to Bahram accidentally killing her in a fit of rage. This tale highlights themes of power, responsibility, and the consequences of rash actions.

keyMyths.title: The Seven Princesses
keyMyths.description: Bahram Gur built seven pavilions, each dedicated to a princess from a different region of the world, and adorned with a specific color. Each princess would tell him a story, offering him wisdom and guidance. These tales are a testament to the importance of diverse perspectives and the value of storytelling.
keyMyths.source: Haft Paykar (Seven Beauties) by Nizami Ganjavi

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.description: The primary source for the legends and stories of Bahram Gur.
sources.text: The primary source for the legends and stories of Bahram Gur.

sources.author: Nizami Ganjavi
sources.description: A romanticized account of Bahram Gur's life and his interactions with seven princesses.
